request.question: E. J. Singler plays for the Canadian G League basketball team based in what city?

sentences: The Maine Red Claws are a professional minor league basketball team based in Portland, Maine.
The Red Claws compete in the NBA G League and are affiliated with the Boston Celtics.
The team played its first season in 2009–10.
The team's home court is the Portland Expo Building.
The team is owned by Maine Basketball, LLC, which is chaired by William Ryan, Jr.
The Red Claws made their first playoff appearance on April 6, 2013, as the eighth seeded team and was swept by the Rio Grande Valley Vipers in the opening round.
The NBA G League All-Star Game is an annual exhibition basketball game held by the NBA G League.
The G League was founded in 2001 as the National Basketball Development League (NBDL) and later as the NBA Development League (D-League).
The league adopted its current name at the start of the 2017–18 season.
The league serves as the National Basketball Association's official minor league basketball organization.
Raptors 905 are a Canadian professional basketball team in the NBA G League.
The team is based in Mississauga, Ontario, and began play in the 2015–16 season.

The club, which is the G League affiliate of the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association, plays their home games at the Hershey Centre, replacing the Centre's former basketball tenant, the Mississauga Power of the National Basketball League of Canada.
The team also plays occasional home games at the Air Canada Centre, the home of their parent club.
Raptors 905 were the eighth D-League team to be owned by an NBA team and the first to be based outside the United States.
The Washington NBA G League team is an American professional basketball team in the NBA G League and an affiliate of the Washington Wizards of the National Basketball Association.
Based in Washington, D.C., the team will play their home games during the 2018–19 season at the St. Elizabeths East Entertainment and Sports Arena.
The team became the twenty-third G League team to be owned by an NBA team.
The NBA G League is the National Basketball Association's official minor league basketball organization.
The league was known as the National Basketball Development League (NBDL) from 2001 to 2005, and the NBA Development League (NBA D-League) from 2005 until 2017.

The league started with eight teams until NBA commissioner David Stern announced a plan to expand the NBA D-League to fifteen teams and develop it into a true minor league farm system, with each NBA D-League team affiliated with one or more NBA teams in March 2005.
At the conclusion of the 2013–14 NBA season, 33% of NBA players had spent time in the NBA D-League, up from 23% in 2011.
As of the 2017–18 season, the league consists of 26 teams, all of which are either single-affiliated or owned by an NBA team.
These are regular season standings and playoff results for the NBA G League.
The NBA G League is the official minor league basketball organization owned and run by the National Basketball Association (NBA).
The league was formed in 2001 as the National Basketball Development League (NBDL).
The league was renamed to NBA Development League (NBA D-League) in 2005 to reflect its close affiliation with the NBA.
In 2017, it was renamed NBA G League, as part of a sponsorship deal with Gatorade.
Edward J. "E. J." Singler (born June 6, 1990) is an American professional basketball player for Raptors 905 of the NBA G League.
He played college basketball for the University of Oregon.
As a senior at Oregon, he was named to the All-Pac-12 first team.
Kyle Edward Singler (born May 4, 1988) is an American professional basketball player for the Oklahoma City Thunder of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
Singler was a four-year starter for the Duke men's basketball team and was instrumental in their 2010 NCAA championship run, earning MVP of the Final Four.
In the 2011 NBA draft, the Detroit Pistons selected Singler in the second round with the overall 33rd pick.
Singler joined the Pistons for the 2012–13 season after playing in Spain during 2011–12.
The Iowa Wolves are an American professional basketball team based in Des Moines, Iowa.
It is owned by and affiliated with the Minnesota Timberwolves as of the 2017–18 season.
They play in the Western Conference in the NBA G League, a minor league basketball organization run by the National Basketball Association (NBA).
The Wolves play their home games at the Wells Fargo Arena.
From 2007 to 2017, the team was known as the Iowa Energy in the NBA Development League (D-League) until being purchased and renamed by the Timberwolves.
They broke the D-league attendance record on their first ever home game with 8,842 fans.
They later set the record again in game two of the 2011 D-League Finals with an attendance of 14,036 fans.
They won the 2011 D-League Finals, defeating the Rio Grande Valley Vipers two-games-to-one.
The Fort Wayne Mad Ants are a team of the NBA G League, located in Fort Wayne, Indiana.
The Allen County War Memorial Coliseum is the home venue for the team.
The team is the first minor league basketball franchise to play in Fort Wayne since the Fort Wayne Fury of the Continental Basketball Association.
The Mad Ants won their first D-League championship in 2014.
In September 2015, the Indiana Pacers purchased the Mad Ants, thus becoming the Pacers' one-to-one D-League affiliate.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: E. J. Singler plays for the Canadian G League basketball team based in what city?
hop1_docs: Kyle Singler | Kyle Edward Singler (born May 4, 1988) is an American professional basketball player for the Oklahoma City Thunder of the National Basketball Association (NBA).  Singler was a four-year starter for the Duke men's basketball team and was instrumental in their 2010 NCAA championship run, earning MVP of the Final Four.  In the 2011 NBA draft, the Detroit Pistons selected Singler in the second round with the overall 33rd pick.  Singler joined the Pistons for the 2012–13 season after playing in Spain during 2011–12.

E. J. Singler | Edward J. "E. J." Singler (born June 6, 1990) is an American professional basketball player for Raptors 905 of the NBA G League.  He played college basketball for the University of Oregon.  As a senior at Oregon, he was named to the All-Pac-12 first team.

Sherri Singler | Sherri Nadine Singler (born February 19, 1974, in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan as Sherri Leonard) is a Canadian curler from Harris, Saskatchewan.  She currently plays second for the Stefanie Lawton team.

Saigon Heat | The Saigon Heat (Vietnamese: "Sài Gòn Heat" ) are a Vietnamese professional basketball team based in Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam.  They play in the ASEAN Basketball League.  Since the 2014 season, the Heat's home games are played at the CIS Arena on the campus of the Canadian International School in District 7.  Saigon Heat is the first professional basketball team based in Vietnam.

Raptors 905 | Raptors 905 are a Canadian professional basketball team in the NBA G League.  The team is based in Mississauga, Ontario, and began play in the 2015–16 season.  The club, which is the G League affiliate of the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association, plays their home games at the Hershey Centre, replacing the Centre's former basketball tenant, the Mississauga Power of the National Basketball League of Canada.  The team also plays occasional home games at the Air Canada Centre, the home of their parent club.  Raptors 905 were the eighth D-League team to be owned by an NBA team and the first to be based outside the United States.

Mississauga Power | The Mississauga Power were a Canadian professional basketball team based in Mississauga, Ontario, that competed in the National Basketball League of Canada (NBL).  Established in as the Oshawa Power, they played in the Central Division.  The Oshawa team began play in the inaugural NBL Canada season, along with the London Lightning, Moncton Miracles, and Summerside Storm and three Premier Basketball League (PBL) teams.

Saint John Riptide | The Saint John Riptide are a Canadian professional basketball team based in Saint John, New Brunswick.  They compete in the National Basketball League of Canada (NBL Canada) as a member of the Atlantic Division.  The Riptide play their home games at Harbour Station.  The Riptide are one of two professional basketball teams from the province of New Brunswick, the other being the Moncton Miracles.  The team was established in 2007 as the Manchester Millrats, an expansion team based in Manchester, New Hampshire, which played in the American Basketball Association (ABA).  They would later move to the Premier Basketball League (PBL) in 2008, relocated to Saint John in 2010, and renamed in 2016.
response.summary_1:
- Passage [2]: "Edward J. 'E. J.' Singler ... is an American professional basketball player for Raptors 905 of the NBA G League."  
- Passage [5]: "Raptors 905 ... The team is based in Mississauga, Ontario" (also notes home games at the Hershey Centre and occasional games at the Air Canada Centre).  
Bridge entity: "Raptors 905" connects E. J. Singler to the team's base city (Mississauga, Ontario). No additional identity links appear necessary; next hop can confirm/report the city "Mississauga" as the team's base.

The club, which is the G League affiliate of the Toronto Raptors of the National Basketball Association, plays their home games at the Hershey Centre, replacing the Centre's former basketball tenant, the Mississauga Power of the National Basketball League of Canada.  The team also plays occasional home games at the Air Canada Centre, the home of their parent club.  Raptors 905 were the eighth D-League team to be owned by an NBA team and the first to be based outside the United States.

2014–15 Mississauga Power season | The 2014–15 Mississauga Power season was the fourth and final season of the franchise in the National Basketball League of Canada (NBL).  It was also their second season in Mississauga.  Following an unsuccessful season with a record of 7–25, the Power folded, resulting in the creation of Raptors 905, a new NBA Development League affiliate to the NBA's Toronto Raptors.

Mississauga Trilliums | The Mississauga Trilliums are a girls lacrosse association based in Mississauga, Ontario, Canada.  The Trilliums are associated with the Mississauga Tomahawks in the Ontario Lacrosse Association and were founded in 2006.  The Trilliums play in the "Ontario Women's Lacrosse" league.

List of corporations based in Mississauga | This is a list of large and international corporations with Canadian headquarters in Mississauga, Ontario, Canada.  Additionally companies founded or based in Mississauga are also included.

Mississauga Jr. Chiefs | The Mississauga Jr.  Chiefs are a Canadian Junior women's ice hockey team based in Mississauga, Ontario.  The Chiefs are members of the Provincial Women's Hockey League of the Ontario Women's Hockey Association.  The 2-time regular season champions, 3-time Alumni Cup playoff runners-up, and 3-time Ontario Intermediate AA champions.

Mississauga Twins | The Mississauga Twins were an independent, minor league baseball team of the semi-pro, Intercounty Baseball League based in Mississauga, Ontario.  They played their home games at Meadowvale Field.  They moved to Burlington, Ontario, in 2011 and became the Burlington Twins.

Mississauga Steelheads | The Mississauga Steelheads are a junior ice hockey team in the Ontario Hockey League, based in Mississauga, Ontario, Canada.  The team was originally known as the Toronto St. Michael's Majors, whose hockey program was founded and operated by St. Michael's College School in 1906.
